channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Supreme Court Voting Rights Ruling Could Fuel New Era of Redistricting Wars
item.description: The expected flood of new congressional maps is likely to produce fewer competitive districts, fewer ways for voters to hold elected officials accountable and more polarized politics.
item.dc:creator: Nick Corasaniti
item.media:credit: Tierney L. Cross/The New York Times
item.media:description: The Supreme Court’s ruling on the Voting Rights Act could unleash another redistricting arms race.

item.title: After Supreme Court Decision, Louisiana Weighs Redrawing House Maps
item.description: The Louisiana secretary of state said the House primary election would be delayed after the court ruled the state’s congressional districts unconstitutional. The Senate primary will go forward on May 16.
item.dc:creator: Emily Cochrane and Nick Corasaniti
item.media:credit: Mark Schiefelbein/Associated Press
item.media:description: Gov. Jeff Landry of Louisiana, a Republican, outside the White House in 2025. Mr. Landry is weighing whether to suspend the state’s May 16 primary to redraw congressional maps.

item.title: How the Voting Rights Decision May Block the Rise of Young Black Leaders
item.description: Black Democrats in the South already face steep challenges when seeking political office. But the Supreme Court’s ruling could be felt for a generation.
item.dc:creator: Rick Rojas
item.media:credit: Rory Doyle for The New York Times
item.media:description: Evan Turnage, a former congressional aide, recently challenged the veteran Democratic lawmaker Bennie Thompson in a Mississippi district vulnerable to being redrawn.

item.title: The Homeland Security Shutdown Ends
item.description: Also, Maine’s governor bows out of a crucial Senate race. Here’s the latest at the end of Thursday.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Cullen
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times

item.title: Trump Picks Nicole Saphier for Surgeon General After Withdrawing Casey Means Nomination
item.description: Dr. Means’s nomination had stalled in part over her views on vaccines. The president said he was instead nominating Dr. Nicole B. Saphier, a radiologist at Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center.
item.dc:creator: Sheryl Gay Stolberg and Dani Blum
item.media:credit: Alex Kent for The New York Times
item.media:description: The withdrawal of Dr. Casey Means’s nomination is bound to be a deep disappointment for the Make America Healthy Again movement.

item.title: Crop Undercount Raises Questions About Reliability of U.S.D.A. Data
item.description: Corn estimates were off by 4.5 million acres last year. A lack of survey responses, not job cuts, led to the miss, the Agriculture Department said.
item.dc:creator: Kevin Draper
item.media:credit: Kathryn Gamble for The New York Times
item.media:description: A cornfield in Earlham, Iowa, last fall. The Agriculture Department botched its estimate for the size of the 2025 corn crop.

item.title: Senate Bans Prediction Markets for Its Members and Staff
item.description: Senators voted unanimously to prohibit betting practices in the chamber after some users made hundreds of thousands of dollars online by accurately predicting U.S. military actions.
item.dc:creator: Minho Kim
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Karsten Moran for The New York Times
item.media:description: Anonymous users of prediction markets like Kalshi can bet on a certain outcome of future events, including major policy decisions of the U.S. government.

item.title: Hegseth Says Iran Cease-Fire Stops Clock for Congressional Approval
item.description: The defense secretary testified on the eve of the 60-day mark of the war, a major statutory deadline for the president to withdraw forces or seek approval from Congress to continue the fight.
item.dc:creator: Megan Mineiro
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times
item.media:description: Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth testified for the second straight day on Capitol Hill on Thursday.

item.title: How Trump’s Iran Blockade Is Complicating a High-Stakes Trip to China
item.description: If President Trump flies to China as planned in May, the primary topic will clearly be the rippling economic effects of a war that Beijing has made clear it viewed as unnecessary.
item.dc:creator: David E. Sanger and Tyler Pager
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: A mural in Tehran last week showing Iran controlling the Strait of Hormuz. China imports about a third of its oil and gas through the waterway.

item.title: Hezbollah Using Fiber-Optic Drones Against Israeli Targets
item.description: The Lebanese militant group is attacking Israeli troops with explosive drones controlled by fiber-optic cables, like those commonly used in the war in Ukraine.
item.dc:creator: Isabel Kershner, Natan Odenheimer, Aaron Boxerman and Adam Rasgon
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Shir Torem/Reuters
item.media:description: An Israeli military artillery unit on the Israeli side of the border with Lebanon on Thursday.

item.title: Iran’s Supreme Leader Signals Plan to Maintain Control Over Strait of Hormuz
item.description: Ayatollah Mojtaba Khamenei said in a statement that Iran would establish “new legal frameworks” for the Strait of Hormuz. He also said his country would retain its nuclear capabilities.
item.dc:creator: Leo Sands
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Arash Khamooshi for The New York Times
item.media:description: Iranians held photos of the country’s supreme leader, Ayatollah Mojtaba Khamenei, at a government-organized march in Tehran on Wednesday.

item.title: Decoding the King: Brits Hear Subtle Rebuke to Trump that Americans Might Miss
item.description: Royal watchers in Britain called the visit of King Charles III to America a master class in understated criticism.
item.dc:creator: Michael D. Shear
item.category: Charles III, King of the United Kingdom
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump with King Charles III at the White House on Tuesday.

item.title: Last on King Charles’s U.S. Tour: A Potluck and a Win for Scottish Whisky
item.description: King Charles III and Queen Camilla had a ceremonial farewell at the White House before attending a block party in Front Royal, Va.
item.dc:creator: Michael D. Shear
item.category: Charles III, King of the United Kingdom
Camilla, Queen Consort of the United Kingdom
item.media:credit: Kenny Holston/The New York Times
item.media:description: The president and the first lady bidding farewell to King Charles III and Queen Camilla on Thursday.

item.title: ‘The Devil Wears Prada 2’ Review: A Rhapsody in Cerulean
item.description: In this sequel, Andy (Anne Hathaway) and Miranda (Meryl Streep) encounter a series of crises that set the stage for a larger, existential catastrophe.
item.dc:creator: Manohla Dargis

item.title: For Many in Maine, No Tears Over Mills’s Exit From Senate Race
item.description: Voters who watched Gov. Janet Mills struggle to gain traction in the Democratic Senate primary said they were unsurprised — and in many cases, relieved — to see her exit.
item.dc:creator: Jenna Russell and Kathleen Tomaselli
item.media:credit: Sophie Park for The New York Times
item.media:description: Voters who had watched Gov. Janet Mills, right, a Democrat, struggle to gain traction in the race said they were largely unsurprised.

item.title: Mills Exit Is a Blow to Schumer as Democrats Question His Strategy
item.description: Senator Chuck Schumer, the New York Democrat and minority leader, considered the Maine governor his top recruit for winning the majority. Critics said her collapse showed he is out of touch with the party’s voters.
item.dc:creator: Annie Karni
item.media:credit: Eric Lee for The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator Chuck Schumer, Democrat of New York and the minority leader, at the Capitol last month.

item.title: Jeffrey Epstein’s Possible Suicide Note Hidden From Public View
item.description: An inmate said he discovered the note after Mr. Epstein was found injured in his jail cell, weeks before his death. It’s now locked in a courthouse.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Weiser, Steve Eder and Jan Ransom

item.title: Epstein Victims’ Pursuit of Justice Finds a New Venue: Albany, N.Y.
item.description: Two victims of Jeffrey Epstein will testify at the State Capitol next week in support of a bill that would enable them to seek punitive damages from his estate.
item.dc:creator: Benjamin Oreskes
item.media:credit: Cindy Schultz for The New York Times
item.media:description: Senator Zellnor Myrie is pushing a bill to make it possible for victims of Jeffrey Epstein to seek punitive damages from Mr. Epstein’s estate.

item.title: Sheinbaum’s Dilemma in Mexico: Defy the U.S. or Arrest an Ally
item.description: The Trump administration wants President Claudia Sheinbaum to arrest a Mexican governor. She is faced with few good options in response.
item.dc:creator: Jack Nicas and Emiliano Rodríguez Mega
item.media:credit: Yuri Cortez/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: President Claudia Sheinbaum of Mexico could arrest one of her party allies, the governor of Sinaloa state, and extradite him to the United States, or defy the U.S. request and protect him.

item.title: Is Yoko Ono Still Our Most Radical Artist?
item.description: In the 1960s, she invited an audience to cut off her clothes. As attacks on women’s rights escalate, “Cut Piece” and other decades-old works of feminist art feel more relevant than ever.
item.dc:creator: Amanda Fortini

item.title: Nigerian Military Runs a Fulani ‘Concentration Camp,’ Group Says
item.description: Amnesty International said about 1,500 people have been detained by the military for three months and that many of those who have died from disease and starvation have been children.
item.dc:creator: Saikou Jammeh and Ismail Auwal
item.category: Human Rights and Human Rights Violations
item.media:credit: Pelumi Salako/Associated Press
item.media:description: Soldiers patrolling in a village in Kwara State, Nigeria, in February. The state has seen a surge in violent attacks in recent months.

item.title: Elon Musk’s A.I. Claims of Danger Face Limits in OpenAI Trial
item.description: One of Elon Musk’s abiding fears is that A.I. could one day threaten humans. But the jurors deciding his suit against OpenAI probably won’t hear about it.
item.dc:creator: Cade Metz and Mike Isaac
item.media:credit: Manuel Orbegozo/Reuters
item.media:description: Elon Musk returned to the witness stand in federal court for a third day on Thursday in what is expected to be a monthlong trial.

item.title: How A.I. Killed Student Writing (and Revived It)
item.description: High school and college teachers are watching students write, in the classroom, in order to protect against the incursion of artificial intelligence.
item.dc:creator: Dana Goldstein and Lauren Lancaster
item.media:credit: Lauren Lancaster for The New York Times

item.title: Three of Nashville’s Finest Writers Explain How Country Hits Get Written
item.description: Shane McAnally, Brandy Clark and Josh Osborne talk about songwriting as a day job and the art of telling simple truths in a single line.

item.title: How Taylor Swift Writes a Song, In Her Own Words
item.description: The artist shares stories behind some of her biggest hits, her love of a “rant bridge” and how life in the public eye informs the stories she tells in her songs.

item.title: A Democratic Version of the Tea Party Is Emerging
item.description: No one should underestimate the white-hot fury of the party’s voters.
item.dc:creator: Michelle Goldberg
item.media:credit: Mark Peterson for The New York Times

item.title: Why Are We Still Driving?
item.description: Confronting the weirdness of a Waymo future.
item.dc:creator: Ross Douthat and Sophia Alvarez Boyd
item.media:credit: Patrick T. Fallon/Agence France-Presse — Getty Images

item.title: Oil Price Hits Wartime High Above $120 a Barrel as Iran War Standoff Continues
item.description: Strong earnings and economic data buck fears that prolonged disruption to Middle East fuel supplies will eventually dent economic growth.
item.dc:creator: Joe Rennison and Gregory Schmidt
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Scott Olson/Getty Images

item.title: Britney Spears Is Charged With D.U.I. Involving Drugs and Alcohol
item.description: Officers in California observed the pop star driving fast and erratically in March. After she was arrested, she checked herself into a treatment facility.
item.dc:creator: Matt Stevens and Julia Jacobs
item.media:credit: Eduardo Munoz/Reuters
item.media:description: Britney Spears was in a conservatorship for 13 years because of what her father said were public mental health struggles and possible substance abuse.

item.title: New Banksy Statue Causes Stir in Central London
item.description: The statue depicts a man marching with a flag that covers his face. It appeared in a section of London near statues of 19th-century British military and colonial figures.
item.dc:creator: Michael Levenson
item.media:credit: Kin Cheung/Associated Press
item.media:description: The artist Banksy appeared to have taken credit for statue of a man holding a flag covering his face that stood in Waterloo Place in London.

item.title: At Least 5 Are Injured in Stabbing at a High School in Washington State
item.description: Foss High School in Tacoma, Wash., was on lockdown on Thursday after a stabbing, a school spokeswoman said.
item.dc:creator: Rylee Kirk and Hannah Ziegler
item.media:credit: Foss High School
item.media:description: The authorities said that at least five people were injured at Foss High School in Tacoma, Wash., on Thursday.
